elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper, we construct new $t$-server Private Information Retrieval (PIR) schemes with communication complexity subpolynomial in the previously best known, for all but finitely many $t$.
elements.sent-2411-002: Our results are based on combining derivatives (in the spirit of Woodruff-Yekhanin~\cite{WY ) with the Matching Vector based PIRs of Yekhanin~\cite{Yekhanin and Efremenko~\cite{Efremenko .}}}
elements.sent-2411-003: Previously such a combination was achieved in an ingenious way by Dvir and Gopi~\cite{DG , using polynomials and derivatives over certain exotic rings, en route to their fundamental result giving the first $2$-server PIR with subpolynomial communication.}
elements.sent-2411-004: Our improved PIRs are based on two ingredients:.
elements.sent-2411-005: We develop a new and direct approach to combine derivatives with Matching Vector based PIRs.
elements.sent-2411-006: This approach is much simpler than that of Dvir-Gopi: it works over the same field as the original PIRs, and only uses elementary properties of polynomials and derivatives.
elements.sent-2411-007: A key subproblem that arises in the above approach is a higher-order polynomial interpolation problem.
elements.sent-2411-008: We show how ``sparse $S$-decoding polynomials", a powerful tool from the original constructions of Matching Vector PIRs, can be used to solve this higher-order polynomial interpolation problem using surprisingly few higer-order evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-009: Using the known sparse $S$-decoding polynomials from~\cite{Efremenko,IS,Cheeetal in combination with our ideas leads to our improved PIRs.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Notably, we get a $3$-server PIR scheme with communication $2^{\Otilde( (\log n)^{1/3 ) }$, improving upon the previously best known communication of $2^{\Otilde( \sqrt{\log n )}$ due to Efremenko~\cite{Efremenko .}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Private Information Retrieval (PIR), introduced in \cite{CGKS by Chor, Kushilevitz, Goldreich and Sudan, is a method for a user to interact with $t$ non-colluding servers and read some part of a database without revealing to the servers which part of the database was read.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Specifically, a database $(a_1, \ldots, a_n) \in \{0,1\}^n$ is known to all the $t$ servers, and the user wants to find the value of $a_\tau$ without revealing any information about $\tau$ to any server.
elements.sent-2411-013: PIRs are typically studied with $t$ being a constant, and we will only consider this case.
elements.sent-2411-014: There is a trivial protocol which works even for $t=1$: some server just sends the entire database to the user -- this uses $n$ bits of communication.
elements.sent-2411-015: With more servers it is (surprisingly) possible to use much less communication.
elements.sent-2411-016: The main problem, which has been extensively studied in the subsequent years, is to design PIR schemes with as little total communication as possible.
elements.sent-2411-017: The first generation of PIR schemes were based on Reed-Muller codes, also known as multivariate polynomial evaluation codes, over finite fields $\F$.
elements.sent-2411-018: We give a quick taste of the most elementary such PIR scheme.
elements.sent-2411-019: The original data $a_1, \ldots, a_n$ is used to specify a multivariate polynomial $F(X_1, \ldots, X_k) \in \F[X_1, \ldots, X_n]$ of degree $t-1$ in $k = n^{1/(t-1)}$ variables by ensuring that the value of $F$ at a certain point $u_i \in \F^k$ equals $a_i$.
elements.sent-2411-020: When the user wants to access $a_\tau$, it chooses a uniformly random line $\ell$ through $u_\tau$, picks points $b_1, \ldots, b_{t}$ on $\ell$, and asks server $j$ for the value of $F$ at $b_j$.
elements.sent-2411-021: Using the fact that uniformly random lines through $u_i$ cover the space $\F^k$ uniformly, we get that each $b_i$ is uniformly distributed, and thus no server learns anything about $\tau$.
elements.sent-2411-022: Using the fact that the restriction $F|_{\ell}$ of the low degree multivariate polynomial $F$ to the line $\ell$ is a low degree univariate polynomial, we get that the univariate polynomial $F|_{\ell}$ can be completely recovered from its values at the $b_i$ -- and thus $a_\tau$, the value of $F$ at $u_\tau$ can be recovered by the user.
elements.sent-2411-023: This gives a PIR scheme with $O(k) = O(n^{1/(t-1) )$ communication, and is nontrivial for $t \geq 3$.}
elements.sent-2411-024: With more sophisticated ideas,~\cite{CGKS gave a $2$-server PIR scheme with communication $O(n^{1/3 )$, and even lower communication for $t$-server PIR for $t \geq 3$.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Further improvements to this were given by~\cite{Amb97,BI01,BIKR02 , leading to a $n^{O(\frac{\log\log t {t \log t )}$-communication protocol for large $t$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Then in 2007, the breakthrough result of Yekhanin~\cite{Yekhanin completely changed the landscape by giving $3$-server PIR schemes with subpolynomial ($n^{O(\frac{1 {\log\log n )}$) communication (assuming the infinitude of Mersenne primes).}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Soon after, building on a greatly clarifying presentation and reinterpretation of~\cite{Yekhanin by Raghavendra~\cite{Raghavendra , a beautiful result of Efremenko unconditionally achieved significantly smaller communication $2^{\Otilde(\sqrt{\log n )}$ for $3$-server PIR, and even lower communication (of the form $2^{\Otilde( (\log n)^{\varepsilon_t ) }$ for more servers).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: There are two key ingredients in these constructions: $S$-Matching Vector Families (SMVF), and Sparse $S$-Decoding polynomials (SSD) for a set $S \subseteq \Z_m$ with $0 \in S$.
elements.sent-2411-029: The challenge is to get as large a collection as possible.
elements.sent-2411-030: Sparse $S$-Decoding polynomials are sparse polynomials that take certain prescribed values at the set of points $ \{ \gamma^{s : s \in S \}$, where $\gamma$ is a primitive $m$th root of $1$ in some field.}
elements.sent-2411-031: The challenge is to get the sparsity (= number of monomials) as small as possible.
elements.sent-2411-032: Yekhanin's original construction took $m$ being prime, and was based on moderately large $S$-Matching Vector Families but astonishingly sparse $S$-decoding polynomials.
elements.sent-2411-033: Efremenko's construction was based on $m$ being composite, and was based on very large $S$-Matching Vector Families (originating in the work of Grolmusz~\cite{Gro and Barrington-Beigel-Rudich~\cite{BBR ) and slightly nontrivially sparse $S$-decoding polynomials.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Subsequent work by Itoh-Suzuki~\cite{IS and Chee-Feng-Ling-Wang-Zhang~\cite{Cheeetal improved the sparsity of $S$-decoding polynomials, which led to quasipolynomial reduction in the communication over Efremenko's result) for $t$-server PIR for all $t \geq 9$.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: We refer to this entire approach as the SMVF+SSD framework.
elements.sent-2411-036: We will give a detailed overview of this framework soon.
elements.sent-2411-037: In the other direction, there are only very weak lower bounds known for PIR.
elements.sent-2411-038: Wehner and de Wolf \cite{WdW showed a $5 \log n$ lower bound on communication for $2$-server PIR.}
elements.sent-2411-039: It is possible (but would be very surprising if so) that there are $2$-server PIR schemes with $O(\log n)$ communication.
elements.sent-2411-040: PIR schemes are closely related to Locally Decodable Codes (LDCs), and progress on both has often come together.
elements.sent-2411-041: Indeed, Reed Muller codes are the original LDCs, and the SMVF+SSD framework is also the engine behind the record holding Matching Vector Code constant query LDCs.
elements.sent-2411-042: In the non-constant query regime, local decoding is still very interesting, and Matching Vector Codes are LDCs in this setting too -- this was shown by Dvir-Gopalan-Yekhanin~\cite{DGY and Ben-Aroya-Efremenko-Ta-Shma~\cite{BET -- whose work also shed further light on the SMVF + SSD framework.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: For the formal connections between PIR schemes and LDCs, see Katz-Trevisan~\cite{KatzTrevisan and Trevisan~\cite{Trevisan04 .}}
elements.sent-2411-044: One basic question that remained open for a while was whether 2-server PIR could be done with subpolynomial communication.
elements.sent-2411-045: This was resolved by the beautiful work of Dvir-Gopi~\cite{DG , which is also the starting point for our work.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Dvir-Gopi developed a way to reduce the number of servers in Matching Vector Family based PIRs by increasing the amount of communication being sent by the servers.
elements.sent-2411-047: This required an ingenious new adaptation of an idea of Woodruff-Yekhanin~\cite{WY , originally for the setting of Reed-Muller code based PIRs, to the setting of Matching Vector Family based PIRs.}
elements.sent-2411-048: The Woodruff-Yekhanin idea in the setting of the Reed-Muller based PIR scheme described earlier, is to make the servers also return all the higher order partial derivatives of the multivariate polynomial $F$ at all the queried points $b_i$.
elements.sent-2411-049: This higher order evaluation information for the multivariate polynomial $F$ will let the user deduce, via the chain rule, higher order evaluations of the univariate degree $t-1$ polynomial $F|_{\ell}$ from each server-- and leads to less servers being needed overall.
elements.sent-2411-050: To get an analogue of this in the setting of Matching Vector Family based PIRs, Dvir-Gopi worked over an exotic ring $R_m = \Z_m[\gamma]/\langle\gamma^m - 1\rangle$ in place of a base finite field, and developed some theory of derivatives and interpolation from higher order evaluations over $R_m$.
elements.sent-2411-051: Not all facts from the finite field case carry over, but enough facts did, and this enabled the reduction of the number of servers needed for subpolynomial communication from $3$ to $2$.
elements.sent-2411-052: For $2$ servers this gives PIR schemes with $2^{\Otilde(\sqrt{\log n )}$ communication.}
elements.sent-2411-053: The method of Dvir-Gopi also generalized to a larger number of servers, and led to communication complexity subpolynomial in the previous best bounds, whenever the number of servers $t$ lies in $\{2\} \cup \{4,5,6,7,8\} \cup \{16, \ldots, 23\}$.
elements.sent-2411-054: In this paper, we construct $t$-server PIR schemes with communication complexity that is subpolynomial in the previously best known bound, for all but finitely many $t$ (in fact, our improvements are for precisely the $t$ where the Dvir-Gopi scheme did not improve the state of the art).
elements.sent-2411-055: A key part of our improvement is a new and simpler method to apply derivatives (in the spirit of Woodruff-Yekhanin) to reduce the number of servers in Matching Vector based PIRs -- and in particular giving a simpler proof of the Dvir-Gopi results.
elements.sent-2411-056: This method works natively over the finite field $\F_p$ and only uses elementary properties of polynomials over finite fields.
elements.sent-2411-057: As a by product of our simpler approach to applying derivatives, we are able to formulate a higher multiplicity analogue of the problem of having a sparse $S$-decoding polynomial.
elements.sent-2411-058: Miraculously, it turns out that whenever there is an unusually sparse $S$-decoding polynomial in the original sense, it automatically translates into an unusually sparse higher multiplicity analogue of the $S$-decoding polynomial -- thus leading to an immediate reduction, using the existing results on sparse $S$-decoding polynomials from~\cite{Efremenko,IS,Cheeetal , in the number of required servers (beyond the improvement that derivatives already gave).}
elements.sent-2411-059: We believe our result is the more natural way to use derivatives to improve the communication in the SMVF+SSD framework.
elements.sent-2411-060: The total communication of our new PIR scheme can be very succinctly stated as follows: {\em If the SMVF+SSD framework yields a $t$-server PIR scheme with communication $\exp(\Otilde((\log n)^{\frac1r )$, then we get a $t$-server PIR scheme with communication $\exp(\Otilde((\log n)^{\frac{1 {r+1 ))$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: In terms of concrete parameters, the net result is that we improve the communication complexity to a subpolynomial in the previously best known, for the state of the art $t$-server PIR for all $t$ except $t \in \{2\} \cup \{4,5,6,7,8 \} \cup \{16, 17, \ldots, 23 \}$ (for these exceptions our bound is no better than that of Dvir and Gopi, although our protocol and the proof of correctness is simpler).
elements.sent-2411-062: For concrete comparison, we give a table listing the number of servers needed to achieve $\exp(\Otilde((\log n)^{1/c ))$ communication for different integers $c$.}
elements.sent-2411-063: We list this below (for a few small values $c$) for our PIR scheme, the Dvir-Gopi scheme and the original Efremenko scheme (using the best known $S$-decoding polynomials from ~\cite{Efremenko,IS,Cheeetal ).}
elements.sent-2411-064: We now give a quick introduction to PIR schemes based on the SMVF+SSD framework, discussing the important ideas of Yekhanin~\cite{Yekhanin , Raghavendra~\cite{Raghavendra , Efremenko~\cite{Efremenko and Dvir-Gopalan-Yekhanin~\cite{DGY .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: Our presentation is slightly more algebraic than usual.
elements.sent-2411-066: For the expert, the main point is that it treats $F( \cdot)$, the multivariate function encoding the data, as a genuine polynomial with natural number exponents, and not as an exponential polynomial with $\Z_m$ exponents.
elements.sent-2411-067: We then consider its restriction to a parametrized curve $C(Z)$, leading to a univariate polynomial $A(Z) = F(C(Z))$ of quite large degree.
elements.sent-2411-068: It is only at this stage where we restrict ourselves to evaluations at $m$th roots of unity, by reducing the univariate polynomial $A(Z)$ mod $Z^m -1$.
elements.sent-2411-069: This change in viewpoint will greatly help when considering derivatives: it is the key reason our PIR scheme and its proof are simpler than that of Dvir-Gopi, and also underlies our improved PIR schemes.
elements.sent-2411-070: Let $\F$ be a field, and let $(a_1, \ldots, a_n) \in \F^n$ be the database for which we want a PIR scheme.
elements.sent-2411-071: The key ingredient that we need is a matching vector family.
elements.sent-2411-072: For an integer $m$ and a set $S \subseteq \Z_m$ with $0 \in S$, an {\em $S$-matching vector family is a collection of pairs $(u_1, v_1), \ldots, (u_n, v_n)$, where each $u_i$ and $v_i$ lies in $\Z_m^k$ such that the inner products obey the following restrictions: \begin{equation}\langle u_i, v_j \rangle \in  \{0\} i = j S \setminus \{0 \} i \neq j .\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\langle u_i, v_j \rangle \in  \{0\} i = j S \setminus \{0 \} i \neq j .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-073: We will also view the $u_i$ and $v_i$ as vectors in $\{0,1,\ldots, m-1\}^k \subseteq \mathbb N^k$.
elements.sent-2411-074: A key fact is that whenever $m$ is composite, superpolynomial size matching vector families exist.
elements.sent-2411-075: Next we choose a field $\F$ of characteristic $p$ relatively prime to $m$ which contains a set $\mgroup$ of all $m$ $m^{\mathrm{th }$ roots of unity.}
elements.sent-2411-076: The database is used to specify a multivariate polynomial $F(\mathbf X) \in \F[X_1, \ldots, X_k]$ as follows: \begin{equation}F(\mathbf X) = \sum_{i = 1}^{n} a_i \mathbf X^{u_i}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-077: The $u_i$, which are a-priori in $\Z_m^k$, are treated here as elements of $\mathbb N^k$.
elements.sent-2411-078: This is part of the change in viewpoint that will help us later.
elements.sent-2411-079: Now if the user wants to recover $a_{\tau}$, it will use $v_\tau$ to pick a random parametrized curve $C(Z) \in (\F[Z])^k$ (a $k$-tuple of univariate polynomials) as follows: for uniformly random ${\bm \beta = (\beta_1, \ldots, \beta_k) \in \mgroup^k$, it sets $C(Z) = (\beta_1 Z^{v_{\tau,1 , \ldots, \beta_k Z^{v_{\tau, k )$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: Because of the randomness of ${\bm \beta}$, for any $h \in \mgroup$, $C(h)$ is distributed uniformly at random in $\mgroup^k$.
elements.sent-2411-081: Thus asking each server for the value of $F$ at a single $C(h)$ will maintain privacy perfectly.
elements.sent-2411-082: This will give us access to values of the composed polynomial $A(Z) = F(C(Z))$ at some points $h \in \mgroup$.
elements.sent-2411-083: The key point in the definition of $C$ is that $A(Z)$ has the following nice form: \begin{equation}A(Z) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i {\bm \beta}^{u_i} Z^{\langle u_i, v_\tau \rangle},\end{equation} where the inner products $\langle u_i, v_\tau \rangle$ are treated as inner products of nonnegative integer vectors (and thus $A(Z)$ is of huge degree $\approx k \cdot m^2$).
elements.sent-2411-084: To utilize the matching vector property, which talks about the inner products $\langle u_i, v_\tau\rangle$ mod $m$ and not the integer $\langle u_i, v_\tau \rangle$, we will only substitute $Z$ to be an element of $\mgroup$.
elements.sent-2411-085: Equivalently, we can talk about the remainder of $A(Z)$ mod the polynomial $Z^m -1$, which has the effect of reducing all the exponents of $Z$ mod $m$.
elements.sent-2411-086: This is the view we take, and this is the point of difference alluded to earlier.
elements.sent-2411-088: By the matching vector property and the above discussion, $A_1(Z)$ is of the form: \begin{equation}A_1(Z) = a_\tau {\bm \beta}^{u_\tau} + \sum_{s \in S \setminus \{0\}} c_s Z^s,\end{equation} and thus the $a_\tau$ we seek can be found from the constant term of $A_1(Z)$.
elements.sent-2411-089: Further, since the polynomial $Z^m-1$ has all the elements of $\mgroup$ as roots, the values of $A_1$ on $\mgroup$ equal the values of $A$ on $\mgroup$.
elements.sent-2411-090: Thus the user gets access to $A_1$ at some points $h \in \mgroup$.
elements.sent-2411-091: What remains for the user is to solve an interpolation problem: {\em given evaluations of a polynomial of the form $A_1(Z)$ at some points in $\mgroup$, find the constant term of $A_1$}.
elements.sent-2411-092: The number of evaluations needed determines the number of servers - and this is where sparse $S$-decoding polynomials come in -- sometimes this interpolation problem can be solved using surprisingly few evaluation points.
elements.sent-2411-093: Now we will see how to improve this using derivatives.
elements.sent-2411-094: Following the idea of Woodruff-Yekhanin in the setting of Reed-Muller code based PIR schemes, Dvir and Gopi~\cite{DG suggested asking the servers for not only the value of $F$ at points $C(h)$, but also for higher order derivatives of $F$ at these points.}
elements.sent-2411-095: The hope was that fewer higher-order evaluations are needed to solve the interpolation problem, and thus fewer servers are needed for the PIR scheme.
elements.sent-2411-097: Most significantly, taking derivatives of a polynomial whose exponents are in $\Z_m$ seemed to require (since $(Z^i)' = i\cdot Z^{i-1}$ -- the $i$ came down from the exponent to the coefficient) the coefficient ring of the polynomials to accommodate elements of $\Z_m$.
elements.sent-2411-098: This was handled by Dvir and Gopi through an ingenious idea -- to replace the coefficient field $\F$ for the polynomials with a {\em ring $R_m$ which by design contains the $m$th roots of $1$ and also $\Z_m$; namely $R_m = \Z_m[\gamma]/\langle \gamma^m -1 \rangle$.}
elements.sent-2411-099: The use of the exotic ring $R_m$ enabled many aspects of this idea to go through; however the interpolation properties of values and derivatives do not behave as nicely as they do over fields.
elements.sent-2411-100: Here~\cite{DG tackle the resulting interpolation problem hands on, and the final proof ultimately relies on nonvanishing (but not noninvertibility!) of some mysterious $4\times 4$ determinants over $R_m$ in the $2$-server case, and an explicit solving of a certain linear system over $R_m$ by reducing to rings of the form $\Z_p[\gamma]/\langle \gamma^m - 1\rangle$ and the Chinese remainder theorem for the general $t$-server case.}
elements.sent-2411-101: We remark that some further simplifications are possible in the $2$-server case.
elements.sent-2411-102: For $2$-servers and when $m = 6$, Dvir-Gopi find a homomorphism from the ring $R_6$ to the field $\F_3$ which preserves the vital nonvanishing determinant in their proof -- thus the entire protocol can be made to work directly over $\F_3$.
elements.sent-2411-103: For a larger number of servers there does not seem to be an analogue of this.
elements.sent-2411-104: In a recent striking result, Alon, Beimel and Lasri~\cite{ABL2024 find an extremely elementary protocol and proof of the $2$-server PIR result of~\cite{DG , without ever mentioning polynomials (but still using Matching Vector Families).}}
elements.sent-2411-105: This is also only for 2 servers.
elements.sent-2411-106: We now describe our approach to using derivatives to improve the communication of the matching vector PIR schemes.
elements.sent-2411-107: It works over the original field $\F$ itself and uses only classical derivatives over fields.
elements.sent-2411-108: We will set $M = mp$ and take the matching vector family $(u_i, v_i)$ over the ring $\Z_{M}$.

elements.sent-2411-110: Since $M$ has one more prime factor than $m$, the size of the matching vector family that we take can be quasipolynomially larger than what can be done in the original Efremenko scheme.
elements.sent-2411-111: We will still only be evaluating $F$ at points of $\mgroup$, the $m^{\rm{th }$ roots of $1$.}

elements.sent-2411-113: It will again pick a random curve $C(Z)$ just as before: $C(Z) = ( \beta_1 Z^{v_{\tau,1 , \ldots, \beta_k Z^{v_{\tau,k )$, where ${\bm \beta \in \mgroup^k$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-114: Thus asking each server for the value and higher order derivatives of $F$ at a single $C(h)$ will maintain privacy perfectly.
elements.sent-2411-115: Via the chain rule for derivatives, this will give the user access to values and higher order derivatives of the the composed polynomial $A(Z) = F(C(Z))$ at some points $h \in \mgroup$.
elements.sent-2411-116: Again, the key point in the definition of $C$ is that $A(Z)$ has the following nice form: \begin{equation}A(Z) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i {\bm \beta}^{u_i} Z^{\langle u_i, v_\tau \rangle}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-117: To utilize the matching vector property, which talks about the inner products $\langle u_i, v_\tau\rangle$ mod $M$ and not the integer $\langle u_i, v_\tau \rangle$, we will reduce $A(Z)$ mod $(Z^M - 1)$.
elements.sent-2411-120: Since we are in characteristic $p$, \begin{equation}Z^M - 1 = Z^{mp}-1^p = (Z^{m} - 1)^p,\end{equation} which vanishes at each of the $m$ points of $\mgroup$ with multiplicity $p$.
elements.sent-2411-121: Thus the evaluations of $A_1(Z)$ {\em and its first $p-1$ derivatives agree with the evaluations of $A(Z)$ and its first $p-1$ derivatives at the points of $\mgroup$.}
elements.sent-2411-122: Since we have access to these higher order evaluations of $A(Z)$ at the points of $\mgroup$, we now find ourself faced with a higher-order interpolation problem: {\em Given higher order evaluations of a polynomial of the form $A_1(Z)$ at some points in $\mgroup$, find the constant term of $A_1$.}
elements.sent-2411-123: The number of higher order evaluations determines the number of servers, and it seems reasonable to hope that we will need fewer higher order evaluations to find the constant term (because we get more information from a higher order evaluation).
elements.sent-2411-124: This brings us to the final ingredient.
elements.sent-2411-125: We want a small set of points in $\mgroup$ which suffice for the above higher order interpolation problem.
elements.sent-2411-126: We show how to get unusually small such sets from unusually small sets of points which suffice for the original interpolation problem -- namely, from sparse $S$-decoding polynomials.
elements.sent-2411-127: This is again done using some algebraic insights; most crucially that the order $p$ evaluation of $A_1(Z)$ at the point $b$ is completely determined by $A_1(Z) \mod (Z-b)^p$, and using the identity $(Z-b)^p = Z^p -b^p$ gives us concrete handle on $A_1(Z) \mod (Z-b)^p$.
elements.sent-2411-128: In order to state our main theorem, we need two quick definitions to set up the infrastructure of the SMVF+SSD framework.

elements.sent-2411-170: In this section, we express the property of having a sparse $S$-decoding polynomial in terms of having a small interpolating set for a certain interpolation problem.
elements.sent-2411-171: We then define a multiplicity version of that interpolation problem that will be useful for the improved PIR scheme, and give a general way to get small interpolating sets for this high multiplicity interpolation problem from small interpolating sets for the original interpolation problem without multiplicities (and thus from sparse $S$-decoding polynomials).
elements.sent-2411-172: We say $B \subseteq \F$ has the $0$-interpolating property for $S$ if there is a map $E: \F^B \to \F$, such that for every polynomial $R(Z)$ of the form \begin{equation}R(Z) = \sum_{s \in S} c_s Z^s,\end{equation} we have $E(R|_B) = R(0)$.
elements.sent-2411-173: In words, the evaluations of $R$ at the points of $B$ determine $R(0)$.
elements.sent-2411-174: We say $B \subseteq \F$ has the $0$-interpolating property with multiplicity $e$ for $S$ if there is a map $E : (\F^e)^B \to \F$ such that for every polynomial $R(Z)$ of the form \begin{equation}R(Z) = \sum_{s \in S} c_s Z^s,\end{equation} we have $E(R^{(<e) |_B) = R(0)$.}
elements.sent-2411-175: In words, the order $e$ evaluations of $R$ at the points of $B$ determine $R(0)$.

elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}\sum_{j \in } e_j R(\mgen^{d_j}) = \sum_{j \in } e_j \sum_{s \in S} c_s \cdot (\mgen^{d_j})^ s = \sum_{s \in S} c_s \sum_{j \in } e_j \cdot (\mgen^s)^{d_j} = \sum_{s \in S} c_s P(\mgen^s) = c_0 P(\mgen^0) + \sum_{s \in S \setminus \{0\}} c_s P(\mgen^s) = c_0.\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-216: In this section, we give our improved PIR scheme.
elements.sent-2411-217: To specify the PIR scheme, we need:.
elements.sent-2411-218: A prime $p$ and a natural number $m$ relatively prime to $p$.
elements.sent-2411-220: An integer $e$ with $1 \leq e \leq p$ -- this will govern the multiplicity of interpolation we need.
elements.sent-2411-221: A finite field $\F$ of characteristic $p$ containing the set $\mgroup$ of $m$ different $m$th roots of $1$.
elements.sent-2411-224: An set $B = \{b_1, \ldots, b_t\} \subseteq \mgroup$ with the $0$-interpolating property with multiplicity $e$ for $S_M$ (over $\F$).
elements.sent-2411-225: When we instantiate this for a given $k$, we would like $B$ to be small (this governs the number of servers) and $n$ to be big (this governs the size of the database that can be handled with $\poly(k)$ communication).
elements.sent-2411-226: As we will see, we will eventually instantiate this by taking an $S_m \subseteq \Z_m$ for which there is a sparse $S_m$-decoding polynomial over $\F$, and then take $S_M = S_m \times \{0,1\} \subseteq \Z_m \times \Z_p \simeq \Z_M$ -- and fortunately there happens to be a large $S_M$-Matching Vector Family for this set $S_M$.
elements.sent-2411-227: Suppose the user is interested in finding the value of $a_\tau$.
elements.sent-2411-228: The PIR scheme works as follows: \begin{enumerate \item The user picks a random ${\bm \beta \in \mgroup^k$.}}
elements.sent-2411-229: Here $v_\tau$ is viewed as a vector in $\{0,1,\ldots, M-1\}^k \subseteq \mathbb N^k$.
elements.sent-2411-231: For every $i \in \{1,\ldots,t\}$, the user sends $C(b_i) \in \mgroup^k$ to server $i$.
elements.sent-2411-232: Each server evaluates $F^{(<e)}$ at the point in $\mgroup^k$ that it received, and returns that to the user.
elements.sent-2411-234: Define $A(Z) = F(C(Z))$, which is a polynomial of degree at most $(M-1)^2k$.
elements.sent-2411-236: Observe that $A_1(Z)$ has degree $< M$ and is of the form: \begin{equation}{\bm \beta}^{u_{\tau}}a_\tau + \sum_{s \in S\setminus \{0\}} w_s Z^s.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-237: The user will find $A_1(0) = {\bm \beta ^{u_{\tau a_\tau$, from which $a_\tau$ can be computed.}}}
elements.sent-2411-238: To do this, using the fact that $B$ is a $0$-interpolating set with multiplicity $e$, it suffices for the user to find $(A_1^{(<e) (b_i))_{i = 1 ^t$.}}
elements.sent-2411-239: How do we find $A_1^{(<e) (b_i)$? We now note that it is the same as $A^{(<e) (b_i)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-240: Indeed, since $(Z-b_i)$ divides $Z^m - 1$, we have that $(Z - b_i)^p$ divides $(Z^m-1)^p = (Z^M - 1)$, and so: \begin{equation}A^{(<p)}(b_i) = A_1^{(<p)}(b_i),\end{equation} and in particular, \begin{equation}A^{(<e)}(b_i) = A_1^{(<e)}(b_i).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-241: Finally $A^{(<e) (b_i)$ can be computed by the user from $F^{(<e) (C(b_i))$ and knowledge of $C$.}}
elements.sent-2411-242: From the chain rule, the user can take the servers' answers, $F^{(<e) (C(b_i))$, and knowledge of $C^{(<e) (b_i)$, to compute $A^{(<e) (b_i)$ for each $i \in $.}}}
elements.sent-2411-243: By the earlier observation, $A^{(<e) (b_i) = A_1^{(<e) (b_i),$ and so the user knows order $e$ evaluations of $A_1$ at each point of $B$.}}
elements.sent-2411-244: Finally, by the $0$-interpolation property with multiplicity $e$ of $B$, the user can recover $A_1(0) = {\bm \beta ^{u_\tau a_\tau$, and thus (since all coordinates of $\beta$ are nonzero) compute $a_\tau$, as desired.}}
elements.sent-2411-245: The protocol described above gives a $t$-server PIR scheme for a database of size $n$ with the communication cost $O(k + k^{e-1 )$.}
elements.sent-2411-246: The above procedure shows that the user can recover the value of $a_\tau$ for any $\tau \in [n]$ with probability $1$.

elements.subsection-2411-002: Instantiating the PIR Scheme: proof of the main theorem
elements.sent-2411-250: We are ready to prove Theorem \ref{thm:main .}
elements.sent-2411-251: Let $m$ be a positive integer with $r$ distinct prime factors, and let $p$ be a prime not dividing $m$.
elements.sent-2411-252: By our assumption about $\F$, we know that $\F$ is a finite field of characteristic $p$ which contains all $m$ $m$th roots of $1$ (i.e., $m | (|\F|-1)$).
elements.sent-2411-253: Let $\mgroup \subseteq \F$ denote this set of $m$th roots of $1$ in $\F$.

elements.sent-2411-255: Use Theorem \ref{thm:MVF to obtain an $S_M$-matching vector family $\Fam$ of size $n$ and dimension $k$, where $k = \exp(\Otilde( ( \log n)^{\frac{1 {r+1 ))$ , and $r + 1$ comes from the fact that $M$ is the product of $r + 1$ distinct primes.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-256: By Lemma \ref{lem:polytointerpolate applied to the $S^*$-decoding polynomial given by our hypothesis, we get a subset $B \subseteq \mgroup$, with $|B| \leq t$, which has the $0$-interpolation property for $S^*$.}
elements.sent-2411-257: We will now apply Lemma \ref{lem:SmtoSM to $B$ to get that it is a $0$-interpolating set with multiplicity $e = 2$.}
elements.sent-2411-258: The key point is that the canonical set for $M$, namely $S_M$, is a cartesian product of the canonical set for $m$, namely $S^*$, under the Chinese remainder isomorphism.
elements.sent-2411-259: Indeed: \begin{equation}0 \in S_M \subseteq \Z_M,\end{equation} and that \begin{equation}\phi(S^* \times \{0, 1\}) = S_M\end{equation} where $\phi$ is the Chinese remainder isomorphism $\phi : \Z_m \times \Z_p \to \Z_M$ and $S^*$ is the canonical set for $m$.
elements.sent-2411-260: Thus by Lemma~\ref{lem:SmtoSM with $e = 2$, we get that $B$ is a $0$-interpolating set of multiplicity $2$ for $S_M$.}
elements.sent-2411-261: Now applying Theorem \ref{thm:PIR-general to $B$ and $\Fam$ (with $e = 2$), we get a PIR scheme with communication $O(k + k^{e-1 ) = O(k) = \exp(\Otilde( ( \log n)^{\frac{1 {r+1 ))$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-262: With the main theorem in hand, we now give a proof of the main corollary which simply plugs in the best known sparse $S$-decoding polynomials.

elements.sent-2411-264: Now by applying Theorem \ref{thm:main , to $p =2, m$, we get the result.}
